virtual.comment: Windows uses ASCII codes for alphanumeric virtual keys here: 0x30 is the ASCII code for '0'.
Windows uses ASCII codes for alphanumeric virtual keys here: 0x31 is the ASCII code for '1'.
Windows uses ASCII codes for alphanumeric virtual keys here: 0x32 is the ASCII code for '2'.
Windows uses ASCII codes for alphanumeric virtual keys here: 0x33 is the ASCII code for '3'.
Windows uses ASCII codes for alphanumeric virtual keys here: 0x34 is the ASCII code for '4'.
Windows uses ASCII codes for alphanumeric virtual keys here: 0x35 is the ASCII code for '5'.
Windows uses ASCII codes for alphanumeric virtual keys here: 0x36 is the ASCII code for '6'.
Windows uses ASCII codes for alphanumeric virtual keys here: 0x37 is the ASCII code for '7'.
Windows uses ASCII codes for alphanumeric virtual keys here: 0x38 is the ASCII code for '8'.
Windows uses ASCII codes for alphanumeric virtual keys here: 0x39 is the ASCII code for '9'.
Windows uses ASCII codes for alphanumeric virtual keys here: 0x51 is the ASCII code for 'Q'.
virtual.name: Q
W
virtual.comment: Windows uses ASCII codes for alphanumeric virtual keys here: 0x57 is the ASCII code for 'W'.

virtual.name: E
virtual.comment: Windows uses ASCII codes for alphanumeric virtual keys here: 0x45 is the ASCII code for 'E'.

virtual.comment: Windows uses ASCII codes for alphanumeric virtual keys here: 0x52 is the ASCII code for 'R'.

virtual.comment: Windows uses ASCII codes for alphanumeric virtual keys here: 0x54 is the ASCII code for 'T'.

virtual.comment: Windows uses ASCII codes for alphanumeric virtual keys here: 0x59 is the ASCII code for 'Y'.

virtual.name: U
virtual.comment: Windows uses ASCII codes for alphanumeric virtual keys here: 0x55 is the ASCII code for 'U'.

virtual.name: I
virtual.comment: Windows uses ASCII codes for alphanumeric virtual keys here: 0x49 is the ASCII code for 'I'.

virtual.name: O
virtual.comment: Windows uses ASCII codes for alphanumeric virtual keys here: 0x4f is the ASCII code for 'O'.

virtual.name: P
virtual.comment: Windows uses ASCII codes for alphanumeric virtual keys here: 0x50 is the ASCII code for 'P'.

virtual.name: A
virtual.comment: Windows uses ASCII codes for alphanumeric virtual keys here: 0x41 is the ASCII code for 'A'.

virtual.name: S
virtual.comment: Windows uses ASCII codes for alphanumeric virtual keys here: 0x53 is the ASCII code for 'S'.

virtual.name: D
virtual.comment: Windows uses ASCII codes for alphanumeric virtual keys here: 0x44 is the ASCII code for 'D'.

virtual.name: F
virtual.comment: Windows uses ASCII codes for alphanumeric virtual keys here: 0x46 is the ASCII code for 'F'.

virtual.name: G
virtual.comment: Windows uses ASCII codes for alphanumeric virtual keys here: 0x47 is the ASCII code for 'G'.

virtual.name: H
virtual.comment: Windows uses ASCII codes for alphanumeric virtual keys here: 0x48 is the ASCII code for 'H'.

virtual.name: J
virtual.comment: Windows uses ASCII codes for alphanumeric virtual keys here: 0x4a is the ASCII code for 'J'.

virtual.name: K
virtual.comment: Windows uses ASCII codes for alphanumeric virtual keys here: 0x4b is the ASCII code for 'K'.

virtual.name: L
virtual.comment: Windows uses ASCII codes for alphanumeric virtual keys here: 0x4c is the ASCII code for 'L'.

iso-#.comment: Distinct HID key for the ISO/UK-style #~ key partially tucked under Enter. It is different from the ISO key next to left Shift (HID 0x64), but many software stacks collapse it onto backslash-like semantics.

evdev.comment: Linux hid-input folds HID keyboard usages 0x31 and 0x32 onto the same KEY_BACKSLASH evdev keycode.

xkb-keycode.comment: Because the Linux kernel maps this HID usage to KEY_BACKSLASH, XKB also sees it through the same BKSL key path.

xkb-keysym.comment: Linux reaches this key through the same KEY_BACKSLASH path, so the XKB symbolic result collapses onto the same backslash meaning.

web.comment: UI Events uses the same Backslash code for both HID 0x31 and HID 0x32 on standard 102-key layouts because many platforms do not distinguish them at the scancode layer.

kmonad.comment: KMonad currently uses the same backslash key name for this physical key rather than a distinct non-US-pound token.

virtual.name: Z
virtual.comment: Windows uses ASCII codes for alphanumeric virtual keys here: 0x5a is the ASCII code for 'Z'.

virtual.name: X
virtual.comment: Windows uses ASCII codes for alphanumeric virtual keys here: 0x58 is the ASCII code for 'X'.

virtual.name: C
virtual.comment: Windows uses ASCII codes for alphanumeric virtual keys here: 0x43 is the ASCII code for 'C'.

virtual.name: V
virtual.comment: Windows uses ASCII codes for alphanumeric virtual keys here: 0x56 is the ASCII code for 'V'.

virtual.name: B
virtual.comment: Windows uses ASCII codes for alphanumeric virtual keys here: 0x42 is the ASCII code for 'B'.

virtual.name: N
virtual.comment: Windows uses ASCII codes for alphanumeric virtual keys here: 0x4e is the ASCII code for 'N'.

virtual.name: M
virtual.comment: Windows uses ASCII codes for alphanumeric virtual keys here: 0x4d is the ASCII code for 'M'.

win-shift.comment: Generic Windows Shift virtual-key identity. The Microsoft about-keyboard-input documentation explicitly shows this flow: 1. keyboard hardware reports a physical key; 2. the keyboard driver converts it to a scan code; 3. Windows posts a message with generic VK_SHIFT in wParam and the scan code in lParam; 4. code extracts the scan code from lParam; 5. MapVirtualKeyW(scanCode, MAPVK_VSC_TO_VK_EX) asks Windows for the more specific virtual key for that scan code; 6. Windows returns VK_LSHIFT or VK_RSHIFT. Keep this unsided Windows emission when that is what was reported, but prefer shift-left or shift-right when sided identity is available.

iso-intl-backslash.comment: Distinct ISO extra key beside left Shift, often labeled with less/greater or backslash-like legends depending on layout.

intl-ro.comment: JIS KEY_RO is primarily a printable key. It has no distinct standard XKB keysym and often produces the printable backslash symbol, so the physical-key-specific mapping is left missing here.

henkan.comment: Japanese conversion key for IME candidate conversion.

kana.comment: Japanese kana-mode key. Its naming and native identity diverge across Linux, Windows, macOS, and remapping tools.

muhenkan.comment: Japanese non-conversion key for cancelling or bypassing IME conversion.

kp-enter.comment: Canonical key retained even though some native stacks collapse it onto another underlying identity.

virtual.comment: Windows exposes keypad Enter through the generic Return virtual key rather than a distinct keypad-enter virtual key.

win-control.comment: Generic Windows Control virtual-key identity. The Microsoft about-keyboard-input documentation explicitly shows this flow: 1. keyboard hardware reports a physical key; 2. the keyboard driver converts it to a scan code; 3. Windows posts a message with generic VK_CONTROL in wParam and the scan code in lParam; 4. code extracts the scan code from lParam; 5. MapVirtualKeyW(scanCode, MAPVK_VSC_TO_VK_EX) asks Windows for the more specific virtual key for that scan code; 6. Windows returns VK_LCONTROL or VK_RCONTROL. Keep this unsided Windows emission when that is what was reported, but prefer ctrl-left or ctrl-right when sided identity is available.

win-alt.comment: Generic Windows Alt virtual-key identity. The Microsoft about-keyboard-input documentation explicitly shows this flow: 1. keyboard hardware reports a physical key; 2. the keyboard driver converts it to a scan code; 3. Windows posts a message with generic VK_MENU in wParam and the scan code in lParam; 4. code extracts the scan code from lParam; 5. MapVirtualKeyW(scanCode, MAPVK_VSC_TO_VK_EX) asks Windows for the more specific virtual key for that scan code; 6. Windows returns VK_LMENU or VK_RMENU. Keep this unsided Windows emission when that is what was reported, but prefer alt-left or alt-right when sided identity is available.

linefeed.comment: Separate historical line-feed key identity. This is distinct from the normal Enter/Return key even though many modern stacks barely expose it.

uncertain-begin.comment: AppKit semantic begin function-character identity, likely representing begin-of-line behavior rather than a distinct physical key.

function-character.name: NSInsertFunctionKey
function-character.comment: AppKit distinguishes Insert via NSInsertFunctionKey. In the virtual-keycode API, both Insert and Help collapse onto kVK_Help.

macro.comment: Dedicated macro-recording or macro-trigger key identity.

xkb-keysym.comment: xkbcommon-keysyms.h defines numbered macro keysyms like XF86Macro1..XF86Macro30, but no direct keysym for KEY_MACRO

kp-sign-change.comment: Calculator-style keypad sign toggle: changes the current numeric entry between positive and negative rather than acting as an arithmetic operator.

pause.comment: Classic keyboard Pause key. On Windows, the special Pause scan code is emitted for this key, while Break is emitted for Ctrl+Pause. GNOME treats the Pause key itself as a media-pause trigger and sends the MPRIS "Pause" action.

autohotkey.name: Pause
autohotkey.comment: AutoHotkey also recognizes CtrlBreak for the classic Windows Ctrl+Pause behavior on this key.

scale.comment: Desktop window-overview / expose-style key rather than a text or numeric key.

hanja.comment: HID LANG2 is dual-use across keyboard ecosystems: Windows and Linux usually expose it as the Korean Hanja conversion key, while Apple Japanese keyboards use the same HID usage for the Eisu key.

intl-yen.comment: Japanese yen key. Some software vocabularies also use yen-like spellings for backslash compatibility, so this key is tracked separately.

virtual.comment: Windows distinguishes this key at the scan-code level, but the available JP layout evidence did not establish a distinct canonical virtual key for IntlYen.

iso-level3-shift.comment: Level-3 shift / AltGr-style modifier key represented by the XKB ISO_Level3_Shift keysym.

evdev.comment: keyd defines iso-level3-shift as a special extra keycode and notes that it is missing from Linux input-event-codes.h.

xkb-keycode.comment: Treat this key primarily through its XKB keysym meaning rather than through a stable evdev/XKB keycode name.

compose.comment: Linux compose key with the standard XKB Multi_key keysym. This is distinct from the application/menu key. Kanata currently resolves its `ContextMenu`/`menu`/`apps`/`comp` name family to KEY_COMPOSE here rather than to KEY_MENU.

stop.comment: Linux KEY_STOP / XKB STOP come from Consumer AC Stop. HID Keyboard/Keypad Stop remains only an informational alternative because Linux does not map it to this keycode.

keyboard-stop.name: Stop
keyboard-stop.comment: Informational alternative only: keyboard-page Stop is a separate HID usage, but Linux does not map it to KEY_STOP here.

xkb-keysym.name: XF86Stop
xkb-keysym.comment: Standard XKB stop/abort semantics also collapse onto Cancel, even though stop keeps XF86Stop as its canonical dedicated keysym here.

menu.comment: Application/context-menu key. Kanata does not currently expose a distinct KEY_MENU mapping in its default key-name table; its `menu`/`apps`/`ContextMenu` family resolves to KEY_COMPOSE instead.

xkb-keysym.comment: XKB reuses XF86Send for both KEY_SEND and KEY_SENDFILE, so send-file keeps this only as an informational alternative.
xkbcommon-keysyms.h defines generic delete keysyms like Delete and KP_Delete, but no dedicated keysym for KEY_DELETEFILE

prog1.comment: System-control key for System App Menu.

web.comment: GNOME treats this as the web-browser launcher key and opens an http: action.

launch-msdos.comment: Legacy DOS-launch key. Linux exposes it as KEY_MSDOS and XKB uses XF86DOS, reflecting an old application-launch identity rather than a normal text or system-control key.

screen-lock.comment: Screen-lock / terminal-lock / screensaver key identity. Linux keeps the legacy KEY_COFFEE name for this same keycode.

bookmarks.comment: Bookmarks/favorites key. Use it for bookmark or saved-item lists rather than browser history.

xkb-keysym.comment: XKB reuses XF86Favorites for both KEY_FAVORITES and KEY_BOOKMARKS, so bookmarks keeps this only as an informational alternative.
xkbcommon-keysyms.h notes: Currently no use case for CD-specific keysyms

media-next.comment: GNOME treats this as the media next-track key and sends the MPRIS "Next" action.

media-prev.comment: GNOME treats this as the media previous-track key and sends the MPRIS "Previous" action.

media-rewind.comment: GNOME treats this as the media rewind key and sends the MPRIS "Rewind" action.

xkb-keysym.comment: xkbcommon-keysyms.h states TODO: unclear function _EVDEVK(0x0aa) KEY_ISO

exit.comment: Exit / quit key. The Linux kernel also maps HID Generic Desktop System Menu Exit (0x01/0x88) and Consumer AC Exit (0x0c/0x204) to the same KEY_EXIT / XKB key. XKB additionally has an active xkeyboard-config mapping to XF86Close, but xkbcommon-keysyms.h defines XF86Exit specifically for KEY_EXIT, so the other mapping is preserved here only for reference.

xf86close.name: XF86Close
xf86close.comment: xkeyboard-config symbols-inet maps KEY_EXIT to XF86Close even though xkbcommon-keysyms.h defines XF86Exit specifically for KEY_EXIT.

xkb-keysym.comment: xkbcommon-keysyms.h states TODO: unclear function _EVDEVK(0x0af) KEY_MOVE
xkbcommon-keysyms.h states TODO: unclear function _EVDEVK(0x0b0) KEY_EDIT

scrollup.comment: Consumer-control key for AC Scroll Up.

scrolldown.comment: Consumer-control key for AC Scroll Down.

mouse-left.comment: Primary pointer button. Distinct from keyboard keys even when remappers expose it in the same vocabulary.

mouse-side.comment: Auxiliary side mouse button. On Windows and AutoHotkey this is the 4th mouse button / XBUTTON1.

mouse-extra.comment: Second auxiliary side mouse button. On Windows and AutoHotkey this is the 5th mouse button / XBUTTON2.

mouse-forward.comment: Forward thumb/button mouse control. Distinct from the keyboard/browser forward key.

mouse-back.comment: Back thumb/button mouse control. Distinct from the keyboard/browser back key.

media-fast-forward.comment: GNOME treats this as the media fast-forward key and sends the MPRIS "FastForward" action.

display-brightness.name: Display Brightness
display-brightness.comment: the kernel maps both HID Consumer 0x65 and 0x71 to KEY_CAMERA

hp.comment: HP-vendor key that Linux maps to KEY_HP.

xkb-keycode.comment: xkbcommon comments KEY_HP out as an unclear HP-specific key and does not expose a canonical XKB keycode name for it.

xkb-keysym.comment: No canonical XKB keysym is defined for KEY_HP.

hp-sound.comment: HP-vendor key that Linux maps to KEY_SOUND. This is distinct from the generic audio-browser key.

xkb-keycode.comment: xkbcommon comments KEY_SOUND out as an unclear HP-specific key and does not expose a canonical XKB keycode name for it.

xkb-keysym.comment: No canonical XKB keysym is defined for KEY_SOUND.

hp-question.comment: HP-vendor key that Linux maps to KEY_QUESTION. This is distinct from display-brightness keys and from the ordinary keyboard question-mark character.

xkb-keycode.comment: xkbcommon comments KEY_QUESTION out as an unclear HP-specific key and does not expose a canonical XKB keycode name for it.

xkb-keysym.comment: No canonical XKB keysym is defined for KEY_QUESTION.

email.comment: Linux exposes KEY_EMAIL separately from KEY_MAIL, and the kernel maps HID Consumer 0x80 to it even though the HID tables name that usage Selection. XKB and Windows flatten the launcher behavior to the generic mail-launch action, so those canonical mappings live under mail instead.

assign.name: Assign
assign.comment: the kernel maps both HID Consumer 0x199 and 0x81 to KEY_CHAT

xkb-keycode.comment: xkbcommon marks KEY_LAST as an unclear function and does not expose a canonical XKB keycode name for it.

xkb-keysym.comment: No canonical XKB keysym is defined for KEY_LAST.

market-monitor-or-finance-browser.name: AL Market Monitor or Finance Browser

alternative-erase.comment: Alternate erase key from the HID keyboard page, distinct from normal Backspace/Delete semantics.

xkb-keysym.comment: xkbcommon-keysyms.h states TODO: unclear function _EVDEVK(0x0de) KEY_ALTERASE

media.comment: GNOME treats this as the generic media launcher key rather than a transport-control key.

battery.comment: GNOME treats this as the battery-status key and shows battery information/OSD.

bluetooth.comment: Bluetooth radio key. GNOME has a dedicated Bluetooth airplane-mode path for Bluetooth-specific rfkill keys; use this for toggling the Bluetooth radio rather than general media control.

wlan.comment: Wireless LAN radio key. Use this for toggling Wi‑Fi/radio state on devices that expose a dedicated WLAN hardware key.

ultra-wideband.comment: Ultra‑Wideband radio key. This is a dedicated wireless-radio hardware key for UWB-capable devices rather than a normal application/media shortcut.

unknown.comment: Canonical placeholder for keys that Linux or other stacks surface only as an unknown or unrecognized key code.

hid.comment: Linux uses KEY_UNKNOWN as the fallback target for HID usages that it does not recognize or map more specifically.

media-video-next.comment: Next video-source key. The kernel meaning is “drive next video source”, so this is for cycling display/video inputs and is distinct from next-track media playback.

virtual.comment: Windows exposes only the generic media next-track virtual key here, not a distinct next-video-source virtual key.

media-video-previous.comment: Canonical key retained even though some native stacks collapse it onto another underlying identity.

virtual.comment: Windows exposes only the generic media previous-track virtual key here, not a distinct previous-video-source virtual key.

brightness-toggle.comment: Consumer-control key for Display Brightness Toggle. Linux aliases this to KEY_DISPLAYTOGGLE.

brightness-auto.comment: Kernel meaning: enable automatic brightness control. Linux aliases KEY_BRIGHTNESS_ZERO to the same keycode, but the kernel comment defines the primary meaning as automatic brightness.

contrast-up.comment: Apple display-contrast increase key exposed through the macOS special-key event API.

hid.comment: No standard HID split contrast-up usage was found in the local reference set. Apple exposes this through NX_KEYTYPE_CONTRAST_UP instead.

contrast-down.comment: Apple display-contrast decrease key exposed through the macOS special-key event API.

hid.comment: No standard HID split contrast-down usage was found in the local reference set. Apple exposes this through NX_KEYTYPE_CONTRAST_DOWN instead.

display-off.comment: Display power key. The kernel meaning is “put the display device into the off state”, so this is for turning the display panel off rather than changing brightness.

wireless-wide-area-network.comment: Wireless Wide Area Network radio key. The kernel explicitly defines this for LTE/UMTS/GSM-style mobile broadband radios; `wimax` is just an alias of the same Linux keycode.

ok.comment: Confirm/accept key, typically the OK/Enter button in a remote-control or D-pad style navigation cluster.

goto.comment: Direct navigation key. On media/remotes this is typically used to jump directly to a channel, title, time, or indexed location.

clear.comment: Clear-entry key. Use it for clearing the current value, typed digits, or current selection rather than general delete/backspace behavior.

sysrq-attention.comment: Legacy extended keyboard control key. Historically distinct from Print Screen even when some PC keyboards colocated the two labels.

prior.comment: Legacy extended keyboard control key on the HID keyboard page.

legacy-return.comment: Legacy extended keyboard control key, distinct from the normal Return/Enter key and keypad Enter.

separator.comment: Legacy extended keyboard control key on the HID keyboard page.

out.comment: Legacy extended keyboard control key on the HID keyboard page.

oper.comment: Legacy extended keyboard control key on the HID keyboard page.

power2.comment: Secondary power key. systemd-logind handles KEY_POWER2 through the same power-button path as KEY_POWER, and also supports a distinct long-press action.

option.comment: Options/menu-options key. Treat it as a device or application options command rather than the keyboard Alt/Option modifier.

info.comment: Information/help browser or OEM information key depending on the source stack.

time.comment: Time-display key. On consumer/media devices this is typically used to show or enter a time/timecode/clock-style view rather than general scheduling logic.

vendor.comment: Vendor-specific consumer key. This is reserved for OEM-defined behavior, so its exact action depends on the device vendor rather than a shared desktop convention.

archive.comment: Archive key. Use it for archive/recording-library style actions such as opening archived media or stored items.

media-channel.comment: Channel-selection key. Use it for changing or entering channels in tuner/set-top-box style interfaces.

favorites.comment: Favorites/bookmarks key. Use it for opening or cycling a favorites list rather than generic “like” behavior.

media-electronic-program-guide.comment: Electronic Program Guide key. Linux exposes this separately as KEY_EPG, while XKB keysyms collapse it to the same XF86MediaSelectProgramGuide symbol used for guide.

media-personal-video-recorder.comment: Personal Video Recorder key. The Linux kernel comment maps this to “Media Select Home”, so it is typically the DVR/PVR home/library key.

multimedia-home-platform.comment: TV-platform key for Multimedia Home Platform (MHP), an interactive digital-television middleware standard. This is a specialized media-device key rather than a general desktop keyboard key.

media-language.comment: Language-selection key. On media devices this is typically used to switch audio language or similar playback language settings.

media-caption.comment: Subtitle / closed-caption key. KMonad also aliases this as `caption`, which matches the media-device closed-caption meaning.

keyboard.comment: Consumer-control key for AL Keyboard Layout.

aspect-ratio.comment: Aspect-ratio switching key for display/video devices.

pc.comment: Source-selection key for switching to a PC/computer input on a display or media device.

media-tv.comment: Source-selection key for switching to a TV tuner/input mode.

media-tv2.comment: Alternate TV/cable source-selection key. KMonad aliases it as `cable`, which fits that secondary TV-input meaning.

media-vcr.comment: Source-selection key for switching to a VCR/video-recorder input or mode.

media-vcr2.comment: Alternate VCR/video-recorder source key. KMonad aliases it as `vcr+`, suggesting a secondary VCR input or related recorder mode.

media-satellite.comment: Consumer-control key for Media Select Satellite.

media-cd.comment: Consumer-control key for Media Select CD.

media-tape.comment: Consumer-control key for Media Select Tape.

media-tuner.comment: Consumer-control key for Media Select Tuner.

player.comment: Media-player source/application key for jumping to a playback device or player mode rather than transport control.

hid.name: AL Audio or Video Capture or Playback

media-dvd.comment: Source-selection key for switching to DVD playback/input mode.

link-phone.comment: Linux key for linking a phone with the host device.

media-select-auxiliary.comment: Linux media-selection key exposed as KEY_AUX. XKB names the corresponding symbolic meaning XF86MediaSelectAuxiliary, suggesting selection of an auxiliary media source or mode rather than a generic auxiliary function.

media-mp3.comment: Linux-only media key exposed as KEY_MP3. Its intended meaning appears to be an MP3 or music-player selector, but the upstream semantics are unclear and there is no confirmed matching HID MP3 usage in the current reference set.

media-audio.comment: Consumer-control key for AL Audio Browser.

media-video.comment: Consumer-control key for AL Movie Browser.

memo.comment: Consumer-control key for Media Select Messages.

calendar.comment: Consumer-control key for AL Calendar or Schedule.

red.comment: Consumer-control key for Red Menu Button.

green.comment: Consumer-control key for Green Menu Button.

yellow.comment: Consumer-control key for Yellow Menu Button.

blue.comment: Consumer-control key for Blue Menu Button.

shuffle.comment: Consumer-control key for random play / shuffle.

next.comment: GNOME treats this as the media next-track key and sends the MPRIS "Next" action.

restart.comment: Restart / reset key. The Linux kernel also maps HID Consumer Restart (0x0c/0x0f) and Generic Desktop System Warm Restart (0x01/0x8f) to the same KEY_RESTART / XKB key, so those alternatives are preserved here for reference.

break.comment: Linux KEY_BREAK is a separate modern input key that maps to XF86Break. It is not the same source as the classic Pause/Break keyboard behavior, where Break is produced as Ctrl+Pause on the Pause key.

previous.comment: GNOME treats this as the media previous-track key and sends the MPRIS "Previous" action.

teens.comment: Linux-only special numeric-group key exposed as KEY_TEEN. The name appears to abbreviate the teen-number group, parallel to digits and twenties, rather than a standard HID or desktop key.

twenties.comment: Linux-only special numeric-group key exposed as KEY_TWEN. The name appears to abbreviate the twenties-number group, parallel to digits and teens, rather than a standard HID or desktop key.

games.comment: Consumer-control key for Media Select Games.

editor.comment: Consumer-control key for AL Text Editor.

graphics-editor.comment: Consumer-control key for AL Graphics Editor.

presentation.comment: Consumer-control key for AL Presentation App.

database.comment: Consumer-control key for AL Database App.

messenger.comment: Consumer-control key for AL Instant Messaging.

display-toggle.comment: Display/input switching key. Use it for toggling display outputs or cycling video sources, matching the web `DisplayToggleIntExt` meaning.

spell-check.comment: Consumer-control key for AL Spell Check.

context-menu.comment: Canonical key retained even though some native stacks collapse it onto another underlying identity.

media-repeat.comment: GNOME treats this as the media repeat key and sends the MPRIS "Repeat" action.

media-10-channels-up.comment: Channel-jump key for moving 10 channels up.

media-10-channels-down.comment: Channel-jump key for moving 10 channels down.

images.comment: Consumer-control key for AL Image Browser.

pickup-phone.comment: Telephony control key for pickup phone.

hangup-phone.comment: Telephony control key for hangup phone.

fn-esc.comment: Linux/platform-driver-defined special Fn-layer key identity, exposed as KEY_FN_ESC on some devices rather than as a standard HID key.

fn-f1.comment: Linux/platform-driver-defined special Fn-layer key identity, exposed as KEY_FN_F1 on some devices rather than as a standard HID key.

fn-f2.comment: Linux/platform-driver-defined special Fn-layer key identity, exposed as KEY_FN_F2 on some devices rather than as a standard HID key.

fn-f3.comment: Linux/platform-driver-defined special Fn-layer key identity, exposed as KEY_FN_F3 on some devices rather than as a standard HID key.

fn-f4.comment: Linux/platform-driver-defined special Fn-layer key identity, exposed as KEY_FN_F4 on some devices rather than as a standard HID key.

fn-f5.comment: Linux/platform-driver-defined special Fn-layer key identity, exposed as KEY_FN_F5 on some devices rather than as a standard HID key.

fn-f6.comment: Linux/platform-driver-defined special Fn-layer key identity, exposed as KEY_FN_F6 on some devices rather than as a standard HID key.

fn-f7.comment: Linux/platform-driver-defined special Fn-layer key identity, exposed as KEY_FN_F7 on some devices rather than as a standard HID key.

fn-f8.comment: Linux/platform-driver-defined special Fn-layer key identity, exposed as KEY_FN_F8 on some devices rather than as a standard HID key.

fn-f9.comment: Linux/platform-driver-defined special Fn-layer key identity, exposed as KEY_FN_F9 on some devices rather than as a standard HID key.

fn-f10.comment: Linux/platform-driver-defined special Fn-layer key identity, exposed as KEY_FN_F10 on some devices rather than as a standard HID key.

fn-f11.comment: Linux/platform-driver-defined special Fn-layer key identity, exposed as KEY_FN_F11 on some devices rather than as a standard HID key.

fn-f12.comment: Linux/platform-driver-defined special Fn-layer key identity, exposed as KEY_FN_F12 on some devices rather than as a standard HID key.

fn-1.comment: Linux/platform-driver-defined special Fn-layer key identity, exposed as KEY_FN_1 on some devices rather than as a standard HID key.

fn-2.comment: Linux/platform-driver-defined special Fn-layer key identity, exposed as KEY_FN_2 on some devices rather than as a standard HID key.

fn-d.comment: Linux/platform-driver-defined special Fn-layer key identity, exposed as KEY_FN_D on some devices rather than as a standard HID key.

fn-e.comment: Linux/platform-driver-defined special Fn-layer key identity, exposed as KEY_FN_E on some devices rather than as a standard HID key.

fn-f.comment: Linux/platform-driver-defined special Fn-layer key identity, exposed as KEY_FN_F on some devices rather than as a standard HID key.

fn-s.comment: Linux/platform-driver-defined special Fn-layer key identity, exposed as KEY_FN_S on some devices rather than as a standard HID key.

fn-b.comment: Linux/platform-driver-defined special Fn-layer key identity, exposed as KEY_FN_B on some devices rather than as a standard HID key.

fn-right-shift.comment: Laptop Fn-layer key identity for right shift when firmware or the OS exposes it as a distinct key.

braille-dot-1.comment: Braille input or routing key for dot 1.

braille-dot-2.comment: Braille input or routing key for dot 2.

braille-dot-3.comment: Braille input or routing key for dot 3.

braille-dot-4.comment: Braille input or routing key for dot 4.

braille-dot-5.comment: Braille input or routing key for dot 5.

braille-dot-6.comment: Braille input or routing key for dot 6.

braille-dot-7.comment: Braille input or routing key for dot 7.

braille-dot-8.comment: Braille input or routing key for dot 8.

braille-dot-9.comment: Braille input or routing key for dot 9.

braille-dot-10.comment: Braille input or routing key for dot 10.

button-configuration.comment: Launch-button-configuration key. HID defines it as AL Launch Button Configuration Tool, so use it for opening hardware-button configuration software rather than for a direct button press action.

action-on-selection.comment: Application-launch HID key for action on selection.

contextual-insert.comment: Application-launch HID key for contextual insert.

contextual-query.comment: Application-launch HID key for contextual query.

hid.name: AL Log or Journal or Timecard

screensaver.comment: GNOME treats this as the screensaver/lock key and locks the session.

assistant.comment: Assistant-launch key for invoking a desktop or system voice/AI assistant.

keyboard-layout-next.comment: Keyboard-layout switching key for advancing to the next active input layout or XKB group.

emoji-picker.comment: Emoji-picker launcher key for opening the system emoji or symbol chooser.

dictate.comment: Dictation launcher key for starting system speech-to-text input.

uncertain-dictate-or-command-control-tog.comment: Windows speech-input mode toggle command. This may overlap semantically with dictate, but the local Windows source describes a broader dictation vs command/control mode switch rather than a direct dictate-launch key.

correction-list.comment: Windows speech-input correction-list command for showing alternate recognitions or corrections.

camera-access-enable.comment: Camera control key for access enable.

camera-access-disable.comment: Camera control key for access disable.

camera-access-toggle.comment: Camera control key for access toggle.

accessibility.comment: System-control key for System Accessibility Binding.

do-not-disturb.comment: System-control key for System Do Not Disturb.

brightness-min.comment: Consumer-control key for Display Brightness Minimum.

brightness-max.comment: Consumer-control key for Display Brightness Maximum.

keyboard-input-assist-previous.comment: Consumer-control key for Keyboard Input Assist Previous.

keyboard-input-assist-next.comment: Consumer-control key for Keyboard Input Assist Next.

keyboard-input-assist-previous-group.comment: Consumer-control key for Keyboard Input Assist Previous Group.

keyboard-input-assist-next-group.comment: Consumer-control key for Keyboard Input Assist Next Group.

keyboard-input-assist-accept.comment: Consumer-control key for Keyboard Input Assist Accept.

keyboard-input-assist-cancel.comment: Consumer-control key for Keyboard Input Assist Cancel.

privacy-screen-toggle.comment: Consumer-control key for Privacy Screen Toggle.

eprivacy-screen-on.comment: Explicit electronic privacy screen on key.

eprivacy-screen-off.comment: Explicit electronic privacy screen off key.

performance-mode.comment: Performance boost / G-Mode key used on some Dell and Alienware keyboards.

fn-lock.comment: Laptop Fn-layer key identity for lock when firmware or the OS exposes it as a distinct key.

hid.comment: USB HID page 0x00 is Undefined; no standard usage definition is published for this usage.

hyper-left.comment: Left hyper key identity where a layout exposes Hyper as a distinct sided modifier.

hyper-right.comment: Right hyper key identity where a layout exposes Hyper as a distinct sided modifier.

extend-selection.comment: Legacy extended editing key. ExSel stands for Extend Selection.

hid.comment: USB HID page 0x00 is Undefined; no standard usage definition is published for this usage.

suspend.comment: System suspend key. systemd-logind treats KEY_SUSPEND as suspend-to-disk / hibernate; KDE binds Qt::Key_Hibernate as “Hibernate”. systemd also supports a distinct long-press action.

hid.comment: USB HID page 0x00 is Undefined; no standard usage definition is published for this usage.
USB HID page 0x00 is Undefined; no standard usage definition is published for this usage.

homepage.comment: GNOME treats this as the home launcher key and invokes its home action.

search.comment: GNOME treats this as the search launcher key and invokes its search action.

katakana.comment: Japanese katakana-mode key. Platform vocabularies do not line up cleanly on this key.

virtual.comment: Windows collapses dedicated Katakana input into the generic IME Kana mode virtual key, so katakana keeps this only as an informational alternative.

hiragana.comment: Japanese hiragana-mode key. Platform vocabularies do not line up cleanly on this key.

file.comment: Consumer-control key for AL File Browser.

calc.comment: GNOME treats this as the calculator launcher key and opens GNOME Calculator.

config.comment: Consumer-control key for AL Consumer Control Configuration.

front.comment: Legacy front-page/front-panel style key from the Linux input namespace. KMonad avoids the alias `select` here because it conflicts with KEY_SELECT, so keep it distinct from the generic select/menu-pick key.

keyboard-illumination-toggle.comment: Keyboard backlight toggle key. GNOME toggles the keyboard-brightness service; KDE binds it as “Toggle Keyboard Backlight”, shows an OSD, and restores keyboard brightness after resume.

keyboard-illumination-up.comment: Keyboard backlight increase key. GNOME steps the keyboard-brightness service up; KDE increases keyboard brightness, shows an OSD, and restores keyboard brightness after resume.

keyboard-illumination-down.comment: Keyboard backlight decrease key. GNOME steps the keyboard-brightness service down; KDE decreases keyboard brightness, shows an OSD, and restores keyboard brightness after resume.

mute-mic.comment: GNOME binds this as the microphone mute key and routes it through the quiet sound-action path for input-device mute, rather than the normal output-volume mute action.

uncertain-microphone-volume-mute.comment: Windows microphone-volume mute command. This may overlap semantically with mute-mic, but the local references do not prove that they are the same underlying key identity.

uncertain-mic-on-off-toggle.comment: Windows microphone on/off toggle command. This may overlap semantically with mute-mic or microphone, but the local references do not prove a shared underlying key identity.

select.comment: Select / menu-pick key. The Linux kernel also maps HID Consumer Menu (0x0c/0x09) and Generic Desktop System Menu Select (0x01/0x89) to the same KEY_SELECT / XKB key, so those alternatives are preserved here for reference. Distinct from KEY_FRONT.

again.comment: Canonical key retained even though some native stacks collapse it onto another underlying identity.

xkb-keysym.comment: XKB reuses Redo for both KEY_REDO and the keyboard-page Again key, so again keeps this only as an informational alternative.

function-character.name: NSHelpFunctionKey
function-character.comment: AppKit distinguishes Help via NSHelpFunctionKey. In the virtual-keycode API, both Help and Insert collapse onto kVK_Help.

execute.comment: Keyboard-page Execute identity. Linux, XKB, web, and Windows APPCOMMAND expose this action as Open, while HID and several toolchains use Execute; this canonical follows the HID naming.

sleep.comment: System sleep key. systemd-logind treats KEY_SLEEP as suspend-to-RAM; KDE binds Qt::Key_Sleep as “Suspend”. Both allow configured system behavior, and systemd also supports a distinct long-press action.

print-command.comment: Consumer-key print command. This is distinct from Print Screen / SysRq: use it for an application-level print action rather than screen capture.

xkb-keysym.comment: XKB commonly reuses Print for KEY_PRINT, but that collapses it with Print Screen / SysRq rather than preserving the distinct consumer print-command semantics.

virtual.comment: Windows exposes Print Screen here, not a distinct consumer print-command virtual key.

control-panel.comment: Settings/control-panel key. GNOME does not bind this exact symbol directly, but it does have a dedicated control-center launcher action for settings-style keys.

media-cd-eject.comment: Optical-media eject key for opening the tray / ejecting the disc.

media-cd-eject-close.comment: Combined optical-drive eject/close key. Linux exposes it separately from plain eject, while higher-level stacks usually collapse it to generic eject semantics.

hid.comment: Linux defines a distinct combined eject/close optical-drive key, but hid-input only maps the standard Consumer Eject usage to KEY_EJECTCD, not to KEY_EJECTCLOSECD.

xkb-keycode.name: I170
xkb-keycode.comment: Linux gives the combined eject/close key its own evdev and XKB keycode, but the higher-level XKB meaning still collapses onto the generic eject behavior.

xkb-keysym.name: XF86Eject
xkb-keysym.comment: XKB collapses the combined eject/close key onto the generic XF86Eject symbolic output.

task-select.comment: Task/application switcher key. The web name `SelectTask` suggests the operating-system task switcher or application-selection surface.

media-stop.comment: Generic media-stop key. Linux historically exposes this HID stop usage as KEY_STOPCD, while XKB presents it via XF86AudioStop.

xkb-keysym.comment: No dedicated standard XKB keysym for KEY_STOPCD; XF86AudioStop is only a generic media-stop approximation rather than a 1:1 equivalent.

cd-play.comment: Optical-media play key. Distinct from the generic media play key when devices expose a dedicated CD playback command.

xkb-keysym.comment: No dedicated standard XKB keysym for KEY_PLAYCD; XF86AudioPlay is only a generic media-play approximation rather than a 1:1 equivalent.

kp-comma.comment: Keyboard-page key for Keyboard Comma and Less Than.

media-guide.comment: Program-guide key. The Linux kernel comment identifies this as “Media Select Program Guide”, so it is the guide/listings key.

zoom.comment: Zoom/view-toggle key. Linux aliases KEY_ZOOM to KEY_FULL_SCREEN, and the web mapping names it ZoomToggle.

airplane-mode.comment: Global radio toggle / airplane-mode key. GNOME toggles airplane mode for it, and the kernel describes it as the key that controls all radios.

apple-dashboard.comment: Apple Dashboard key on the Apple vendor keyboard page.

evdev.comment: Linux treats Apple Dashboard as the same evdev key identity used for All Applications. KEY_DASHBOARD is an alias of KEY_ALL_APPLICATIONS.

xkb-keycode.comment: Linux maps the collapsed Dashboard/All Applications evdev identity to the same XKB key as all-applications.

xkb-keysym.comment: Linux exposes Dashboard through the same XKB keysym used for all-applications.

apple-launchpad.comment: Apple Vendor Keyboard key for Launchpad.

apple-spotlight.comment: Apple Vendor Keyboard key for Spotlight.

apple-show-desktop.comment: Apple Show Desktop / Expose Desktop key on the Apple vendor keyboard page.

apple-video-mirror.comment: Apple video-mirroring key on the Apple vendor top-case page.

evdev.comment: Linux maps Apple Video Mirror to the same evdev key identity used for display-toggle.

xkb-keycode.comment: Linux exposes Apple Video Mirror through the same XKB key as display-toggle.

xkb-keysym.comment: Linux exposes Apple Video Mirror through the same XKB keysym used for display-toggle.

fn.comment: Generic Fn identity as exposed by Chromium/Web using the HID System Function Shift usage.

apple-fn.comment: Apple top-case Fn key exposed on Apple's vendor-defined top-case HID page.

apple-function.comment: Apple vendor-keyboard Function key exposed on Apple's vendor-defined keyboard HID page.

terminal-or-run.comment: Application-launch HID key for a terminal or run-command interface.

hid.name: AL Command Line Processor or Run

digital-rights-manager.comment: Application-launch key for Digital Rights Manager software or settings. This is a specialized consumer-device launcher rather than a general keyboard or media-transport key.

oem-help.comment: Application-launch HID key. keyd upstream exposes the Linux OEM-help key as hp, which is treated here as vocabulary for this canonical OEM-help key rather than as a separate canonical key.

hid.name: AL Preemptive Halt Task or Application
AL Remote Networking or ISP Connect
AL Smart Card Information or Help

kp-equals-as400.comment: Distinct from standard keypad equals; HID defines a separate keypad-equals usage for AS/400 keyboards.

bass-down.comment: Consumer audio-control key for lowering bass.

bass-up.comment: Consumer audio-control key for raising bass.

treble-down.comment: Consumer audio-control key for lowering treble.

treble-up.comment: Consumer audio-control key for raising treble.

locking-caps-lock.comment: Legacy mechanical-locking Caps Lock usage, distinct from ordinary Caps Lock.

locking-num-lock.comment: Legacy mechanical-locking Num Lock usage, distinct from ordinary Num Lock.

locking-scroll-lock.comment: Legacy mechanical-locking Scroll Lock usage, distinct from ordinary Scroll Lock.

loudness.comment: Consumer audio-control key for loudness mode.

menu-consumer.comment: Consumer-page menu key, distinct from keyboard-menu usages.

evdev.comment: Linux hid-input maps HID Consumer 0x40 to KEY_MENU, the same evdev key used for Keyboard Application.
Linux hid-input maps HID Consumer 0x43 to KEY_DOWN.
Linux hid-input maps HID Consumer 0x46 to KEY_ESC.
Linux hid-input maps HID Consumer 0x44 to KEY_LEFT.
Linux hid-input maps HID Consumer 0x45 to KEY_RIGHT.
Linux hid-input maps HID Consumer 0x42 to KEY_UP.
Linux hid-input maps HID Consumer 0x48 to KEY_KPMINUS.
Linux hid-input maps HID Consumer 0x47 to KEY_KPPLUS.

picture-in-picture-swap.comment: Consumer display-control key for picture-in-picture source swapping.

picture-in-picture-toggle.comment: Consumer display-control key for picture-in-picture mode.

evdev.comment: Linux defines KEY_3D_MODE, but hid-input does not map HID Consumer 0x6e to that evdev key.

help-center.comment: Application-launch HID key for an integrated help center.

evdev.comment: Linux hid-input maps HID Consumer 0x1a6 to KEY_HELP.

oem-feature-browser.comment: Application-launch HID key for an OEM feature browser.

evdev.comment: Linux hid-input maps HID Consumer 0x1bd to KEY_INFO.

microphone.comment: Consumer microphone-control key. Linux folds this usage into brightness-up, but that is not a semantic match.

evdev.comment: the kernel maps HID Consumer 0x0004 to KEY_BRIGHTNESSUP

xkb-keycode.comment: Linux exposes this through the same XKB key as brightness-up after the kernel fold.

xkb-keysym.comment: Linux exposes this through the same XKB keysym as brightness-up after the kernel fold.

international7.comment: Region- or vendor-specific keyboard usage. These International7+ keys are part of the HID keyboard page but are rarely present on actual modern keyboards.

international8.comment: Region- or vendor-specific keyboard usage. These International7+ keys are part of the HID keyboard page but are rarely present on actual modern keyboards.

international9.comment: Region- or vendor-specific keyboard usage. These International7+ keys are part of the HID keyboard page but are rarely present on actual modern keyboards.

lang6.comment: Region- or vendor-specific keyboard usage. These Lang6+ keys are part of the HID keyboard page but are rarely present on actual modern keyboards.

lang7.comment: Region- or vendor-specific keyboard usage. These Lang6+ keys are part of the HID keyboard page but are rarely present on actual modern keyboards.

lang8.comment: Region- or vendor-specific keyboard usage. These Lang6+ keys are part of the HID keyboard page but are rarely present on actual modern keyboards.

lang9.comment: Region- or vendor-specific keyboard usage. These Lang6+ keys are part of the HID keyboard page but are rarely present on actual modern keyboards.

gamepad-south.comment: Canonical south face-button identity for modern gamepads. Linux defines this position explicitly; Windows exposes an A-labeled button that usually occupies this location on Xbox-style pads.

hid.comment: Raw HID button numbers are not a reliable cross-device way to identify the south face-button position.

virtual.name: VK_GAMEPAD_A
virtual.comment: Windows names this button A, but the virtual-key table does not itself prove the physical south position.

gamecontroller.name: ButtonA
gamecontroller.comment: Apple GameController exposes this on GCExtendedGamepad. Apple's documented face-button diamond places A at the south position.

gamepad-east.comment: Canonical east face-button identity for modern gamepads. Linux defines this position explicitly; Windows exposes a B-labeled button that usually occupies this location on Xbox-style pads.

hid.comment: Raw HID button numbers are not a reliable cross-device way to identify the east face-button position.

virtual.name: VK_GAMEPAD_B
virtual.comment: Windows names this button B, but the virtual-key table does not itself prove the physical east position.

gamecontroller.name: ButtonB
gamecontroller.comment: Apple GameController exposes this on GCExtendedGamepad. Apple's documented face-button diamond places B at the east position.

gamepad-north.comment: Canonical north face-button identity for modern gamepads. Linux defines this position explicitly; Windows exposes an X-labeled button that usually occupies this location on Xbox-style pads.

hid.comment: Raw HID button numbers are not a reliable cross-device way to identify the north face-button position.

virtual.name: VK_GAMEPAD_X
virtual.comment: Windows names this button X, but the virtual-key table does not itself prove the physical north position.

gamecontroller.name: ButtonY
gamecontroller.comment: Apple GameController exposes this on GCExtendedGamepad. Apple's documented face-button diamond places Y at the north position.

gamepad-west.comment: Canonical west face-button identity for modern gamepads. Linux defines this position explicitly; Windows exposes a Y-labeled button that usually occupies this location on Xbox-style pads.

hid.comment: Raw HID button numbers are not a reliable cross-device way to identify the west face-button position.

virtual.name: VK_GAMEPAD_Y
virtual.comment: Windows names this button Y, but the virtual-key table does not itself prove the physical west position.

gamecontroller.name: ButtonX
gamecontroller.comment: Apple GameController exposes this on GCExtendedGamepad. Apple's documented face-button diamond places X at the west position.

gamepad-left-shoulder.comment: Left upper shoulder button on a standard gamepad.

evdev.name: BTN_TL
evdev.comment: Linux uses TL as the terse name for the left upper shoulder button.

gamepad-right-shoulder.comment: Right upper shoulder button on a standard gamepad.

evdev.name: BTN_TR
evdev.comment: Linux uses TR as the terse name for the right upper shoulder button.

gamepad-left-trigger.comment: Left lower trigger button on a standard gamepad.

evdev.name: BTN_TL2
evdev.comment: Linux uses TL2 as the terse name for the left lower trigger button.

gamepad-right-trigger.comment: Right lower trigger button on a standard gamepad.

evdev.name: BTN_TR2
evdev.comment: Linux uses TR2 as the terse name for the right lower trigger button.

gamepad-select.comment: Left-side menu-pad button on a standard gamepad. Windows VIEW is treated as the best available match, though the label differs.

hid.comment: Linux HID maps Generic Desktop Select directly through HID_GD_SELECT to BTN_SELECT, but this file stores the canonical cross-platform identity rather than a single raw HID usage.

virtual.name: VK_GAMEPAD_VIEW
virtual.comment: Windows documents this as the View/Back button; this is the closest standard counterpart to Linux BTN_SELECT.

gamecontroller.name: ButtonOptions
gamecontroller.comment: Apple GameController exposes ButtonOptions as the secondary menu button on GCExtendedGamepad. This is the closest mac-native counterpart to the select/back role.

gamepad-start.comment: Right-side menu-pad button on a standard gamepad. Windows MENU is treated as the best available match.

hid.comment: Linux HID maps Generic Desktop Start directly through HID_GD_START to BTN_START, but this file stores the canonical cross-platform identity rather than a single raw HID usage.

virtual.name: VK_GAMEPAD_MENU
virtual.comment: Windows documents this as the Menu/Start button; this is the closest standard counterpart to Linux BTN_START.

gamecontroller.name: ButtonMenu
gamecontroller.comment: Apple GameController exposes ButtonMenu as the primary menu button on GCExtendedGamepad. This is the closest mac-native counterpart to the start/menu role.

gamepad-home.comment: Branded third system button distinct from the ordinary select/back and start/menu buttons. Linux gamepad naming maps this to BTN_MODE and describes it with examples including Nintendo HOME, the Xbox center logo button, and the PlayStation PS button. SDL gamepad naming models the same control as GUIDE. Chromium's standard gamepad mapping calls the same semantic slot META.

evdev.name: BTN_MODE
evdev.comment: The Linux gamepad specification uses BTN_MODE for the branded special button, with examples including Nintendo HOME, the Xbox center logo button, and PlayStation PS.

virtual.comment: The Win32 VK_GAMEPAD_* table does not expose a separate virtual key for this third system button. In other vocabularies, Linux gamepad naming maps it as BTN_MODE, SDL gamepad naming calls it GUIDE, and Chromium's standard gamepad mapping calls it META.

gamecontroller.name: ButtonHome
gamecontroller.comment: Apple GameController exposes ButtonHome as the special Home-style system button on GCExtendedGamepad.

gamepad-left-thumbstick-button.comment: Digital press of the left thumbstick.

gamepad-right-thumbstick-button.comment: Digital press of the right thumbstick.

gamepad-dpad-up.comment: Up direction on a gamepad D-pad.

hid.comment: Devices may expose D-pad state either as buttons or as hat axes.

evdev.name: BTN_DPAD_UP
evdev.comment: Some Linux devices expose the same logical direction through ABS_HAT0Y instead of or in addition to BTN_DPAD_UP.

mac.comment: Apple GameController exposes the DPad as a directional element rather than four separately named button properties. Map this in directional-handling code rather than as a raw key identity in keys.json.

gamepad-dpad-down.comment: Down direction on a gamepad D-pad.

hid.comment: Devices may expose D-pad state either as buttons or as hat axes.

evdev.name: BTN_DPAD_DOWN
evdev.comment: Some Linux devices expose the same logical direction through ABS_HAT0Y instead of or in addition to BTN_DPAD_DOWN.

mac.comment: Apple GameController exposes the DPad as a directional element rather than four separately named button properties. Map this in directional-handling code rather than as a raw key identity in keys.json.

gamepad-dpad-left.comment: Left direction on a gamepad D-pad.

hid.comment: Devices may expose D-pad state either as buttons or as hat axes.

evdev.name: BTN_DPAD_LEFT
evdev.comment: Some Linux devices expose the same logical direction through ABS_HAT0X instead of or in addition to BTN_DPAD_LEFT.

mac.comment: Apple GameController exposes the DPad as a directional element rather than four separately named button properties. Map this in directional-handling code rather than as a raw key identity in keys.json.

gamepad-dpad-right.comment: Right direction on a gamepad D-pad.

hid.comment: Devices may expose D-pad state either as buttons or as hat axes.

evdev.name: BTN_DPAD_RIGHT
evdev.comment: Some Linux devices expose the same logical direction through ABS_HAT0X instead of or in addition to BTN_DPAD_RIGHT.

mac.comment: Apple GameController exposes the DPad as a directional element rather than four separately named button properties. Map this in directional-handling code rather than as a raw key identity in keys.json.

gamepad-left-thumbstick-up.comment: Upward directional abstraction for the left thumbstick. Windows exposes this as a virtual key, while Linux normally reports the left stick as ABS_X and ABS_Y axes rather than discrete buttons.

evdev.comment: Linux typically reports this through negative ABS_Y on the left stick rather than a discrete button code.

mac.comment: Apple GameController exposes LeftThumbstick as a directional element with analog tilt. Map this in axis-threshold handling code rather than as a raw key identity in keys.json.

gamepad-left-thumbstick-down.comment: Downward directional abstraction for the left thumbstick. Windows exposes this as a virtual key, while Linux normally reports the left stick as ABS_X and ABS_Y axes rather than discrete buttons.

evdev.comment: Linux typically reports this through positive ABS_Y on the left stick rather than a discrete button code.

mac.comment: Apple GameController exposes LeftThumbstick as a directional element with analog tilt. Map this in axis-threshold handling code rather than as a raw key identity in keys.json.

gamepad-left-thumbstick-right.comment: Rightward directional abstraction for the left thumbstick. Windows exposes this as a virtual key, while Linux normally reports the left stick as ABS_X and ABS_Y axes rather than discrete buttons.

evdev.comment: Linux typically reports this through positive ABS_X on the left stick rather than a discrete button code.

mac.comment: Apple GameController exposes LeftThumbstick as a directional element with analog tilt. Map this in axis-threshold handling code rather than as a raw key identity in keys.json.

gamepad-left-thumbstick-left.comment: Leftward directional abstraction for the left thumbstick. Windows exposes this as a virtual key, while Linux normally reports the left stick as ABS_X and ABS_Y axes rather than discrete buttons.

evdev.comment: Linux typically reports this through negative ABS_X on the left stick rather than a discrete button code.

mac.comment: Apple GameController exposes LeftThumbstick as a directional element with analog tilt. Map this in axis-threshold handling code rather than as a raw key identity in keys.json.

gamepad-right-thumbstick-up.comment: Upward directional abstraction for the right thumbstick. Windows exposes this as a virtual key, while Linux normally reports the right stick as ABS_RX and ABS_RY axes rather than discrete buttons.

evdev.comment: Linux typically reports this through negative ABS_RY on the right stick rather than a discrete button code.

mac.comment: Apple GameController exposes RightThumbstick as a directional element with analog tilt. Map this in axis-threshold handling code rather than as a raw key identity in keys.json.

gamepad-right-thumbstick-down.comment: Downward directional abstraction for the right thumbstick. Windows exposes this as a virtual key, while Linux normally reports the right stick as ABS_RX and ABS_RY axes rather than discrete buttons.

evdev.comment: Linux typically reports this through positive ABS_RY on the right stick rather than a discrete button code.

mac.comment: Apple GameController exposes RightThumbstick as a directional element with analog tilt. Map this in axis-threshold handling code rather than as a raw key identity in keys.json.

gamepad-right-thumbstick-right.comment: Rightward directional abstraction for the right thumbstick. Windows exposes this as a virtual key, while Linux normally reports the right stick as ABS_RX and ABS_RY axes rather than discrete buttons.

evdev.comment: Linux typically reports this through positive ABS_RX on the right stick rather than a discrete button code.

mac.comment: Apple GameController exposes RightThumbstick as a directional element with analog tilt. Map this in axis-threshold handling code rather than as a raw key identity in keys.json.

gamepad-right-thumbstick-left.comment: Leftward directional abstraction for the right thumbstick. Windows exposes this as a virtual key, while Linux normally reports the right stick as ABS_RX and ABS_RY axes rather than discrete buttons.

evdev.comment: Linux typically reports this through negative ABS_RX on the right stick rather than a discrete button code.

mac.comment: Apple GameController exposes RightThumbstick as a directional element with analog tilt. Map this in axis-threshold handling code rather than as a raw key identity in keys.json.

uncertain-win-oem-clear.comment: Windows OEM Clear virtual-key identity. Windows documents this as a Clear key, but it is not proven to be the same key identity as VK_CLEAR or keypad clear.

uncertain-win-packet-input.comment: Windows VK_PACKET identity used to pass Unicode characters as if they were keystrokes for non-keyboard input methods.

uncertain-win-oem-8.comment: Windows OEM-specific VK_OEM_8 identity. The Windows table notes that it varies by keyboard and gives a Canadian CSA Right Ctrl example, which is not enough to prove a stable canonical key identity.
